notebook_title: Pokémon vs. Digimon: Why Larger N and Smaller |H| — An Interactive Tour of ML Generalization
learning_objectives: Recap the three-step ML framework (function set, loss, optimization) and define model complexity as the number of candidate functions |H|.
Build a one-parameter threshold classifier on the Pokémon/Digimon edge-count feature and compute its loss (error rate) on a dataset.
Distinguish the ideal hypothesis h_all found on the universe D_all from the empirical h_train found on a sampled D_train, and measure the resulting generalization gap.
Explain what makes a training set 'good' (the uniform-deviation condition with epsilon = delta/2) and estimate the probability of sampling a bad D_train by Monte Carlo simulation.
Derive and visualize the generalization bound P(D_train is bad) <= |H| * 2 * exp(-2 * N * epsilon^2) from the union bound and Hoeffding's inequality.
Quantify the roles of sample size N and model complexity |H|, including the sample-complexity rule N >= log(2|H|/delta) / (2*epsilon^2) and the bias-complexity tradeoff that motivates deep learning.

cells.goal: Title, motivation (Pokémon vs. Digimon classifier), and an explicit list of learning objectives plus a roadmap of the notebook so learners know the destination: understanding why 'larger N, smaller |H|' makes a training set trustworthy.

Set up the environment: import numpy, matplotlib, and ipywidgets; configure inline plotting; fix a random seed for reproducibility; define small plotting helpers reused throughout. This is the single source of shared libraries and the RNG.
cells.widget_plan: none — environment setup; verifies ipywidgets is importable so later interactive cells run in Colab

cells.goal: Review the basic idea of ML in three steps (Step 1: function with unknown parameters; Step 2: define loss; Step 3: optimization) and introduce model complexity as |H| = number of candidate functions. Pose the guiding question: 'more parameters → easier to overfit. Why?'
cells.widget_plan: none — conceptual recap with the central question to be answered empirically later

cells.goal: Introduce the case study: classify an image as Pokémon or Digimon using a single hand-crafted feature, the number of edges e (Digimon have more complex lines → larger e). Define the threshold classifier f_h: predict Pokémon if e >= h else Digimon, with hypothesis set H = {1, 2, ..., 10000} so |H| = 10000.
cells.widget_plan: none — sets up the feature and hypothesis set that the code cells will instantiate

cells.goal: Construct a synthetic 'universe' D_all that stands in for all Pokémon and Digimon: draw edge-count values e from two overlapping integer distributions (Pokémon centered lower, Digimon centered higher) with controllable overlap, and attach labels. Expose a make_universe() factory and materialize e_all, y_all. This is the data foundation reused by every downstream computation.
cells.widget_plan: none — deterministic data generation (seeded); a static labeled scatter/strip preview of the two classes confirms class overlap

cells.goal: Make the threshold classifier tangible: plot the edge-count histograms of the two classes and let the learner drag a threshold h to see the decision regions and the resulting error rate L(h, D_all) update live. Builds intuition that loss is a function of the chosen hypothesis.
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets IntSlider for threshold h (over EDGE_RANGE); interact() redraws overlaid class histograms, the threshold line, shaded decision regions, and a live error-rate annotation

cells.goal: Formalize loss and find the ideal hypothesis: define f_h(e) and the loss L(h, D) = mean indicator error (note cross-entropy is an alternative), sweep h across the full H, plot the loss curve L(h, D_all) vs h, and mark h_all = argmin and its value L(h_all, D_all). Establishes the 'best on the universe' baseline that generalization is measured against.
cells.widget_plan: none — single static loss-vs-threshold curve with h_all annotated; defines the loss machinery (f_h, loss) reused by all later cells

cells.goal: Show the gap between ideal and reality: sample D_train of size N i.i.d. from D_all, find h_train = argmin L(h, D_train), and display L(h_train, D_train), L(h_train, D_all), and L(h_all, D_all) side by side. A resample button lets learners see how different draws yield different h_train and different generalization gaps (reproducing the 'good D_train1 / bad D_train2' contrast from the lecture).
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets IntSlider for N plus a 'Resample D_train' Button; on interaction it draws a fresh D_train, overlays L(h, D_train) vs L(h, D_all), marks h_train and h_all, and prints the gap L(h_train, D_all) - L(h_all, D_all)

cells.goal: State the generalization goal precisely: we want L(h_train, D_all) - L(h_all, D_all) <= delta. Derive the sufficient 'good training set' condition: for all h in H, |L(h, D_train) - L(h, D_all)| <= epsilon with epsilon = delta/2, and walk through the chained inequality showing why this guarantees the goal. Stress that the argument is model-agnostic, distribution-free, and loss-agnostic.
cells.widget_plan: none — derivation in markdown/LaTeX bridging the empirical demo (C08) and the probabilistic bound (C10–C12)

cells.goal: Estimate the probability of sampling a bad training set: repeat the i.i.d. sampling M times, and for each draw compute the worst-case deviation max over h in H of |L(h, D_train) - L(h, D_all)|; flag the draw as bad if it exceeds epsilon. Report the empirical P(D_train is bad) and show a histogram of worst-case deviations. Lets learners feel how N, epsilon, and the effective |H| change the failure rate.
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets sliders for N, epsilon, number of trials M, and an |H| control (subsample the threshold grid); interact() recomputes the empirical bad-probability and redraws the worst-deviation histogram with the epsilon cutoff marked

cells.goal: Derive the theoretical bound: 'D_train is bad' means at least one h has deviation > epsilon, so by the union bound P(bad) <= sum over h of P(bad due to h); then Hoeffding's inequality gives P(bad due to h) <= 2*exp(-2*N*epsilon^2), yielding P(D_train is bad) <= |H| * 2 * exp(-2*N*epsilon^2). Conclude that larger N and smaller |H| both shrink the bound.
cells.widget_plan: none — step-by-step derivation in markdown/LaTeX that the next code cell makes quantitative

cells.goal: Compare theory to experiment: plot the Hoeffding/union bound |H|*2*exp(-2*N*epsilon^2) as a function of N and overlay the Monte Carlo empirical P(D_train is bad) from C10. Show the bound is valid (always above the empirical curve) but often loose, and that increasing N or decreasing |H| drives both down. Reproduce the lecture's numeric examples as labeled points.
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets sliders for |H| and epsilon; interact() redraws the bound-vs-N curve (log-scaled y) with the empirical points overlaid and annotations for the N=100/500/1000 example values

cells.goal: Turn the bound into a design tool: implement the sample-complexity rule N >= log(2*|H|/delta) / (2*epsilon^2) and let learners enter |H|, delta, and epsilon to read off the required number of training examples, reproducing the lecture result (|H|=10000, delta=0.1, epsilon=0.1 → N>=610).
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets sliders/inputs for |H|, delta, epsilon; interact() prints the required N and plots required N vs epsilon (and/or vs |H|) so the sensitivity is visible

cells.goal: Reveal the bias-complexity tradeoff: sweep model complexity |H| and plot two competing curves — L(h_all, D_all) (the ideal loss, which falls as |H| grows) and the expected generalization gap (which rises as |H| grows) — and their sum, the expected test loss L(h_train, D_all), exposing the U-shape. This answers C03's overfitting question and motivates the closing 'Yes, Deep Learning'.
cells.widget_plan: ipywidgets IntSlider for N; interact() recomputes ideal loss, generalization gap, and expected test loss across a range of |H| values and redraws the three-curve tradeoff plot with the optimal complexity marked

cells.goal: Summarize the key results (loss as a function of h; ideal h_all vs empirical h_train; good-training-set condition; P(bad) <= |H|*2*exp(-2*N*epsilon^2); sample complexity; bias-complexity tradeoff), recap the 'larger N, smaller |H|' guidance and its tension, end on the lecture's punchline (魚與熊掌可以兼得嗎? Yes, Deep Learning), and link to the original course videos and data sources for further study.

global_flow_notes: Linear, top-to-bottom executable narrative. C02 is the single setup cell (libraries + seeded RNG + helpers) every code cell depends on transitively. C05 builds the synthetic universe D_all; C07 defines the reusable loss machinery (f_h, loss) and the h_all baseline. The notebook then escalates: tangible threshold/error-rate intuition (C06), the ideal-vs-empirical gap (C08), the formal 'good training set' goal (C09), an empirical failure-probability estimate (C10), the union-bound + Hoeffding derivation (C11), theory-vs-experiment comparison (C12), the sample-complexity design tool (C13), and finally the bias-complexity tradeoff (C14) that closes the loop on C03's overfitting question. Markdown cells (C01, C03, C04, C09, C11, C15) carry the pedagogy; code cells (C02, C05, C06, C07, C08, C10, C12, C13, C14) make each idea executable and, where marked, interactive. Every interactive cell reuses f_h/loss from C07 and the universe from C05 so widgets stay consistent and re-runnable. Variables flow forward only (no backward references), guaranteeing a clean kernel-restart-and-run-all.
assumptions: No upstream 01_concepts.json existed, so concepts were extracted directly from course_source/MLBasic.pdf (Hung-yi Lee, '淺談機器學習原理 / Pokémon vs. Digimon classifier'); the prioritized concepts are the three-step ML framework, model complexity |H|, loss/error rate, D_all vs D_train and the generalization gap, the good-training-set (uniform-deviation) condition, the union bound + Hoeffding inequality bound, sample complexity, and the bias-complexity tradeoff.

The lecture uses real Pokémon/Digimon edge-count statistics that are not shipped with the repo, so the notebook generates a seeded synthetic 'universe' D_all with the same qualitative structure (two overlapping edge-count distributions, Digimon skewed higher); this keeps the notebook self-contained and fully executable in Colab while preserving every pedagogical point.

Loss is the 0/1 error rate bounded in [0,1] (consistent with the Hoeffding assumption in the slides); cross-entropy is mentioned as an alternative but not used, so the empirical probabilities match the theoretical bound's range assumption.

Interactivity is delivered with ipywidgets (interact, IntSlider, Button), which is available in Google Colab; static fallbacks are implied for non-interactive renders but the design targets a live kernel.

Model complexity |H| is treated as the discrete count of candidate threshold functions (per the slides' 'everything in a computer is discrete' note); VC-dimension is intentionally out of scope, matching the lecture.

Monte Carlo estimates of P(D_train is bad) use a finite number of trials M, so the empirical curve is an approximation that should sit below the Hoeffding bound but may be noisy for small M; this is called out in the relevant cell goals.
